1: Psalm 124, attributed to David, is a communal thanksgiving for deliverance from a threat that is described vividly but without historical specification — the deliberate anonymity makes the psalm reusable across the community's generations. "If the LORD had not been on our side (lule YHWH shehayah lanu) — let Israel say it now (yomar-na Yisra'el)" — the liturgical prompt invites the congregation to corporate recital. "Let Israel say it now" is a call-and-response structure; the community participates in the confession that YHWH's presence made the difference. The conditional particle lule (if not, had it not been for) opens an imagined counter-history of what would have happened without divine intervention — and the imagination of the disaster that did not happen makes the thanksgiving for the deliverance that did happen more intense.
2: "If the LORD had not been on our side when our enemies rose up against us" — the repetition of v1's opening line intensifies through liturgical reduplication. "Our enemies rose up (qum aleinu adam)" — qum (to rise up) with the preposition "against" is the military or legal posture of the adversary rising to attack, accuse, or overwhelm. Adam (humanity, human beings) — not supernatural enemies but human adversaries. The acknowledgment that human power without divine counter-action would have sufficed to destroy the community is the covenant confession of human weakness: Israel's survival is not self-explanatory from a human-power analysis but requires the theological category of divine presence on Israel's side.
3: "They would have swallowed us whole (hayyim bila'unu) when their fury blazed against us" — bala (to swallow, to engulf, to consume entirely) is used of the earth swallowing Korah, Dathan, and Abiram alive (Num 16:30-33), of Sheol swallowing the living (Prov 1:12; Isa 5:14), and of the great fish swallowing Jonah (Jon 1:17). The chaos-monster imagery runs through all these uses: the threat is not merely defeat but total consumption, dissolution into the chaos-realm. "Swallowed alive (hayyim)" — the horror is the vitality of the victim at the moment of engulfing: not the dead body disposed of but living persons consumed. The "fury (haron) blazing" — the incendiary anger as the heat that drives the swallowing — creates an overwhelming image of existential threat that would have been total without YHWH's intervention.
4: "The rushing waters (mayim) would have swept us away; the current (nahalah) would have poured over us" — the imagery shifts from the consuming mouth (v3) to the consuming flood. The water imagery (vv. 4-5) draws on the primordial chaos-water tradition: the flood that overwhelms ordered creation (Gen 6-8), the threatening sea that YHWH subdues at creation (Gen 1:2; Ps 93:3-4), the waters of the lament tradition (Ps 69:1-2; 144:7). A nahal (wadi, seasonal stream) in the Negev can go from bone-dry to catastrophic flash-flood within minutes when distant rains fall — the "current that poured over us" was viscerally familiar to anyone in Palestine: sudden, overwhelming torrents with no warning were one of the landscape's genuine mortal hazards. The natural world's danger maps onto the social danger of overwhelming enemies.
5: "The raging waves (hamayim hazedonim) would have overwhelmed us completely" — zedonim are literally "the insolent, presumptuous, proud waters" — waters that have exceeded their appointed boundaries. In OT creation theology, the waters are given their place and commanded not to cross it (Job 38:8-11; Ps 104:7-9; Prov 8:29; Jer 5:22). "Insolent waters" that have overflowed their limits are waters that have rejected the divine ordering of creation — they represent not merely a natural disaster but a cosmic rebellion against order. The enemies who threaten the community are like these presumptuous waters: they have exceeded the bounds of the divinely permitted, and the divine response is to restrain them as YHWH restrains the sea.
6: "Praise be to the LORD — he did not hand us over as prey (teref) to their teeth!" — the predatory-animal image follows the swallowing, flood, and bird-snare images of the psalm: YHWH has prevented the community from becoming teref (torn meat, prey ripped by a predator's teeth). The blessing of YHWH is articulated as negation: "He did not..." — the deliverance is defined by the catastrophe that did not occur. This structure of praise-through-negation (praising what YHWH prevented rather than what YHWH accomplished) is a distinctive feature of the deliverance thanksgiving: the most intense gratitude often arises from contemplating the disaster averted. Baruk YHWH (praised/blessed be the LORD) — the blessing formula that acknowledges YHWH as the source of all good and the averted disaster as his gift.
7: "We have gotten away like a bird that slips out of the hunter's trap (pah yoqshim) — the snare is broken (hannipah), and we are free!" — the bird-and-snare image is one of the Psalter's most vivid escape metaphors (also Pss 91:3; 141:9; Prov 6:5; 7:23). The trap-setter (yaqush) is the professional fowler who spreads his net or springs his snare — a skill requiring patience, concealment, and knowledge of the bird's habits. The pah (snare, spring-trap) snapped shut on the bird's foot. When the snare breaks, the bird escapes not by strength but by the mechanical failure of the trapping device: freedom comes not from the bird's power but from the failure of what held it. YHWH's intervention is precisely this snare-breaking: the trap set by the enemy is neutralized from outside, and the community escapes because the device that held them has been broken by the divine hand.
8: "Our help comes from the name of the LORD (beshem YHWH), the maker of heaven and earth" — the closing verse echoes Ps 121:2 nearly exactly ("my help comes from the LORD, the maker of heaven and earth"), functioning as a liturgical refrain that bookends the collection's opening. The addition "in the name of" emphasizes the shem (name) of YHWH — which encompasses his character, his acts, and his entire history of faithfulness. To say "our help is in the name of YHWH" is to invoke that entire covenant history as the ground of present confidence. "Maker of heaven and earth" closes the psalm with the same cosmic scope with which it opened: the God who delivered from human enemies is the God who made everything those enemies stood on.
